concise_definition: Horns offense : Basketball offensive strategy which emphasizes spacing to create scoring opportunities near the basket or from the perimeter by way of various actions such as basketball screens and dribble handoffs.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text:
The Horns offense is an offensive scheme that opens up the low post areas with great spacing to create scoring opportunities via the use of offensive actions such as pick and rolls and off-ball screens. The Horns offense is also utilized at various levels of basketball including high school all the way to the professional levels such as the NBA, WNBA, or FIBA.

Table of Contents

- What is the Horns offense in basketball?

- What is an example of a Horns basic play?

- What is an example of a Horns backdoor play?

- What is an example of a Horns continuity play?

- What is an example of a Horns flare play?

- What is an example of a Horns flex play?

- What is an example of a Horns handoff play?

- What is an example of a Horns high post play?

- What is an example of a Horns pick and roll play?

- What are the general advantages of the Horns offense?

sections.heading: What is the Horns offense in basketball?
sections.text: The Horns offense is a basketball offensive strategy that generally focuses on creating scoring opportunities via an alignment or formation that includes one offensive player at the top, two additional players at the high post elbows, and another two players in the corners.

sections.heading: What is another term for the Horns offense
sections.text: The Horns offense is sometimes referred to as the A-set alignment, the A-set offense, or simply the A-set. Essentially, if a line is drawn, starting at one corner player, continuing through both high post players, and ending up at the opposite corner player, that would form the letter A on the court.

sections.heading: How to understand the player roles and diagrams on this page
sections.text:
In reference to any diagrams below, each of the offensive players are generally represented by the standard basketball positions in number format, unless otherwise noted.

In essence, 1 is thepoint guard; 2 is theshooting guard; 3 is thesmall forward; 4 is thepower forward; 5 is thecenter.

It should also be mentioned that it’s possible for players to switch locations during the initial alignment. For example, the small forward and the point guard could switch places, which would have the point guard in one of the corners while the small forward initiates the offense at the top.

Nevertheless, the overall premise remains the same: one player at the top, two players at the elbows, and two players in the corners.

Additionally, in terms of a brief legend for the diagrams:

- Black Dot or Small Circle:The black dot or small circle represents the basketball.

- Solid Arrow:The solid arrow indicates movement or a cut on the basketball court.

- Dotted Arrow:The dotted arrow represents a basketball pass.

- Wavy Arrow:The wavy arrow indicates dribbling.

- T-Shaped Symbol:The T-shaped symbol signifies a screen, also known as a pick.

- Double pipe symbol:The double pipe symbol denotes handoff action.

sections.heading: What is an example of a Horns basic play?
sections.text:
This is an example of a basic Horns basketball play that features ball screens from the high post players as well as roll and pop action, respectively.

[Image Alt Text: The basic Horns offense play featuring ball screens and roll/pop action.]

[Image Alt Text: The basic Horns offense play featuring ball screens and roll/pop action.]

To begin, 1 has the option to use the on-ball screen of either 4 or 5.

For this case, 1 uses the screen set by 5 and dribbles toward the basket from the right side of the court. Following that, 5 rolls to the rim and could receive the ball from 1.

In addition, 3 could receive the ball from 1 and take the open three-point shot.

What’s more, 4 could pop to the top and also be a potential recipient, which could lead to another three-point shot.

Otherwise, 1 could simply retain possession of the ball and take the mid-range shot instead.

It should also be noted that if 1 chose the screen of 4, then all of the actions would be similar. The difference though is that those actions would be on the other side of the floor.

Therefore, 4 would roll and possibly receive the ball. Alternatively, 2 could receive it instead and take the three-point shot if open.

sections.heading: What is an example of a Horns backdoor play?
sections.text:
This is an example of aHorns backdoor playthat also features other actions such as the flex screen to create a high percentage scoring opportunity.

This particular backdoor play from the Horns set is derived from the Los Angeles Lakers NBA team.

sections.heading: Part 1: Backdoor cut
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Backdoor cut from part 1 of a Horns offense play, which could lead to a scoring opportunity such as a layup.]

[Image Alt Text: Backdoor cut from part 1 of a Horns offense play, which could lead to a scoring opportunity such as a layup.]

To begin, 4 receives the ball from 1 who then cuts over the top to set a wide pin down screen.

However, 2 rejects that screen andbackdoor cutsto the basket looking to receive the ball from 4, which could lead to a possible layup.

Nevertheless, if 2 is not open, then 2 continues the cut through the lane to the right side low post area while 1 occupies the vacant left side wing and then receives the ball from 4 instead.

Related:Lakers Horns Strong Hand Off – YouTube

sections.heading: Part 2: Flex and double screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Flex and double screen within part 2 of a Horns backdoor cut play that could result in a layup or three-point shot.]

[Image Alt Text: Flex and double screen within part 2 of a Horns backdoor cut play that could result in a layup or three-point shot.]

Next, 3 cuts to the rim via the flex screen set by 2 and could receive the ball from 1 to potentially score via a layup.

As that happens, 2 cuts to the top by way of the double screen set by 4 and 5, which is alsoscreen the screeneraction.

From there, 2 could receive the ball from 1 and take the three-point shot if open.

sections.heading: What is an example of a Horns continuity play?
sections.text:
This is an example of a Horns continuity play that primarily emphasizes creating scoring options via offensive patterns on both sides of the floor.

What’s more, this particular continuity pattern utilizes anelevator screenin addition to wing isolation action to create possible scoring opportunities around the perimeter or near the basket. It could be potentially useful for teams with three-point shooters and/or teams that have perimeter players that are above average in terms of slashing to the basket.

It is also derived fromEncyclopedia of the Elevation Offenseby Tyler Whitcomb.

sections.heading: Part 1: Dribble entry and elevator screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Dribble entry and elevator screen action within part 1 of a Horns continuity play.]

[Image Alt Text: Dribble entry and elevator screen action within part 1 of a Horns continuity play.]

To begin, 1 executes adribble entrytowards the right side wing. When that occurs, 3 initially cuts toward the basket and after that, 3 cuts to the top via the elevator screen set by 4 and 5.

sections.heading: Part 2: Wing isolation option
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Wing isolation option within Part 2 of a Horns continuity play.]

[Image Alt Text: Wing isolation option within Part 2 of a Horns continuity play.]

Next, while isolated on the wing, 1 could use one or more dribbling moves to get around the on-ball defender X1 and drive to the basket, preferably near the baseline side. Afterwards, 1 could score at the rim if possible.

Additionally, if X2 slides into the paint from the weak side to providehelp defense, then 2 could receive the ball from 1 via adrift pass. Following that, 2 could take the three-point shot if open.

Furthermore, if X4 decides to execute a defensivecloseouton 2 shown via the gray arrow, then 4 could pop out towards the wing area, receive the ball from 2, and then take the three-point shot if feasible.

sections.heading: Part 3: Opposite dribble entry, elevator, and wing iso option
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Dribble entry, elevator screen, and wing isolation action on the opposite side of the floor within Part 3 of a Horns continuity play.]

[Image Alt Text: Dribble entry, elevator screen, and wing isolation action on the opposite side of the floor within Part 3 of a Horns continuity play.]

Conversely, if 1 does not drive towards the basket from the right side wing, then 3 could receive the ball from 1, which triggers the continuity pattern.

From there, if 3 happens to be open after going through the elevator screen, then 3 could take the three-point shot.

Otherwise, 3 executes a dribble entry towards the left side wing area. When that happens, 2 initially cuts toward the basket and then, 2 cuts to the top via the elevator screen set by 4 and 5.

Next, 3 could drive towards the basket, preferably near the baseline while 1 executes a drift cut towards the right side corner. Following that, 3 could potentially score at the rim with a layup or dunk.

Moreover, although it is not shown on the diagram, if the defender who is guarding 1 were to slide towards the lane to provide help defense, then 1 could receive the ball from 3 via the drift pass and take the jump shot if open.

In addition to that, if 3 does not drive to the basket, then 2 could receive the ball from 3. From that point, 2 could take the open shot or dribble towards the right side wing to execute the continuity pattern again.

sections.heading: What is an example of a Horns flare play?
sections.text:
This is an example of a horns flare play that mainly features aflare screenamong other offensive actions, which could lead to three-point shots or additional scoring opportunities around the basket.

[Image Alt Text: A horns flare play that features a flare screen as well as a cross screen, which could result in an open jump shot or scoring opportunity around the basket.]

[Image Alt Text: A horns flare play that features a flare screen as well as a cross screen, which could result in an open jump shot or scoring opportunity around the basket.]

To start, 3 cuts to the left side wing via the wide pin down screen set by 5 who also moves to the left side low post area after the screening action.

Next, 3 receives the ball from 1 and from there, 1 cuts to the right side wing via a flare screen set by 4.

3 throws askip passback to 1 who has an opportunity to take the three-point shot.

If 1 does not shoot, then 5 could cut across the lane to the right side low post area via the cross screen set by 2 who also happens to fill the left side corner subsequent to the screening action.

Following that, 5 could receive the ball from 1 and attempt to score via a low post move.

sections.heading: What is an example of a Horns flex play?
sections.text: This is an example of a Horns flex play that implements theflex screensand other actions such as the pick and roll, which could lead to different scoring possibilities.

sections.heading: Part 1: Pick and roll flex
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Pick and roll action followed by a flex screen within part 1 of a Horns flex play.]

[Image Alt Text: Pick and roll action followed by a flex screen within part 1 of a Horns flex play.]

To start, 1 dribbles toward the right side wing via the pick set by 4 who also rolls to the basket.

The first scoring option is 4 receiving the ball from 1 and if that is not open, then 4 cuts across the lane to set a flex screen with 3 as the recipient.

The second scoring option is 3 using the flex screen and cutting to the basket to receive the ball from 1.

If neither option is available, then 4 fills the left side wing, 3 occupies the right side low post area, and 5 pops out toward the top.

sections.heading: Part 2: Ball reversal and flex screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Ball reversal and flex screen action within part 2 of a Horns flex play.]

[Image Alt Text: Ball reversal and flex screen action within part 2 of a Horns flex play.]

Next,ball reversalaction occurs as 1 reverses the ball to 5 and then 5 reverses it again to 4.

Following that, 2 cuts to the basket via the flex screen set by 3 who also replaces at the right side corner.

Afterwards, 2 could receive the ball from 4 and score via a layup.

If that is not open, then 2 could continue to cut through to the left side corner.

sections.heading: Part 3: Stagger screen and slip option
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: A stagger screen and slip option within part 3 of a Horns flex play, which could result in an open jump shot or layup.]

[Image Alt Text: A stagger screen and slip option within part 3 of a Horns flex play, which could result in an open jump shot or layup.]

Once 2 fills the left side corner, 3 cuts to the top via a stagger screen set by 1 and 5.

From there, 3 could receive the ball from 4 and take the open three-point shot.

Alternatively, if the defender guarding 5 attempts to switch the screen, then 5 could slip to the basket, receive the ball from 4 instead, and score via a layup or dunk.

sections.heading: What is an example of a Horns handoff play?
sections.text: This is an example of a Horns handoff play that also includes other offensive actions such as thescreen the screenertactic to create scoring opportunities.

sections.heading: Part 1: High post handoff
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: A high post handoff within part 1 of a Horns handoff play, potentially resulting in a high percentage layup at the rim.]

[Image Alt Text: A high post handoff within part 1 of a Horns handoff play, potentially resulting in a high percentage layup at the rim.]

To begin, 4 receives the ball from 1 who also cuts through on that same side to potentially receive the ball again via a handoff.

If 1 receives the ball, then 1 could simply dribble towards the basket and score via a layup.

On the other hand, if 1 is not open to receive the ball, then 1 could simply cut through the lane to the left side low post area for the time being.

sections.heading: Part 2: Dribble handoff
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Dribble handoff action within part 2 of a Horns handoff play.]

[Image Alt Text: Dribble handoff action within part 2 of a Horns handoff play.]

Next, 4 performs adribble handoffwith 2 who then dribbles back toward the right slot area that is above the three-point line between the top and the wings.

In addition, 4 occupies the replaces within the empty right corner after executing the dribble handoff action.

sections.heading: Part 3: Flex and screen the screener
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Flex and screen the screener action within Part 3 of a Horns handoff play.]

[Image Alt Text: Flex and screen the screener action within Part 3 of a Horns handoff play.]

Continuing the overall action, 3 cuts to the basket via the flex screen set by 1. Following that, 3 could receive the ball from 2 and score around the basket.

Furthermore, as that happens, 1 could cut towards the left slot area via the down screen set by 5, which is also screen the screener action.

If 3 is not open, then as another alternative option, 1 could receive the ball from 2 and then possibly take the open three-point shot.

sections.heading: What is an example of a Horns high post play?
sections.text:
This is an example of aHorns high post playthat generally emphasizes getting the ball into the elbow area to produce possible scoring opportunities via actions such as off-ball screens.

[Image Alt Text: A high post play within Horns offense that demonstrates a double flare screen and slip action.]

[Image Alt Text: A high post play within Horns offense that demonstrates a double flare screen and slip action.]

To begin, 4 receives the ball from 1 at the left side high post area.

Following that, 3 and 5 set a double flare screen and as that occurs, 1 cuts toward the right side wing area.

Afterwards, 1 receives the ball again from 4 and takes the three-point shot.

Also, if the defender who would be guarding against 3 attempts toswitchthe screen, then 3 couldslipto the basket, receive the ball from 4 instead, and score via a layup.

sections.heading: What is an example of a Horns pick and roll play?
sections.text: This is an example of a Horns pick and roll play that could lead to a possible scoring opportunity such as a layup or dunk.

sections.heading: Part 1: High pick and roll
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: High pick and roll action within Part 1 of a Horns pick and roll play that could potentially lead to a layup.]

[Image Alt Text: High pick and roll action within Part 1 of a Horns pick and roll play that could potentially lead to a layup.]

To start, 1 dribbles toward the right side via the high pick set by 4. Afterwards, 4 rolls to the basket and could gain possession of the ball from 1, which could lead to a layup at the rim.

Otherwise, if 4 is not open, then 1 could retreat back to the adjacent wing area while 4 fills the left side corner and 3 lifts up to the left side wing area.

Additionally, 5 could simply fill the vacant top area for the time being.

sections.heading: Part 2: Side pick and roll
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: Side pick and roll action within Part 2 of a Horns pick and roll play, which could result in another scoring opportunity at the rim.]

[Image Alt Text: Side pick and roll action within Part 2 of a Horns pick and roll play, which could result in another scoring opportunity at the rim.]

Next, 5 cuts toward the right side wing area to set a side pick. 1 uses that pick and dribbles toward the middle. After that, 5 rolls to the basket while 2 lifts up to the right side wing viashake action.

From there, 1 could pass the ball to one of the other teammates for possible scoring opportunities if open. Alternatively, 1 could simply retain possession of the ball and take the mid-range jump shot.

sections.heading: Creation of exceptional spacing via the Horns alignment
sections.text:
The Horns offense and its A-set type of alignment feature exceptionalspacing, particularly between the high post players and corner players on each respective side of the half court.

Within the Horns set, each player is positioned at least 12-15 feet from each other, which makes it easier to perform various offensive actions such as backdoor cuts and dribble penetration via picks.

What’s more, due to the high-level spacing, it is generally more challenging for the defense to provide solid help or even double-team without leaving an offensive player open, which could result in an uncontested jump shot.

sections.heading: Potential neutralization of shot blockers by opening up the low post areas
sections.text:
The Horns offense opens up the low post areas, which could potentially keep low post defenders, especially shot blockers, of the opposing team away from the basket.

That opening can neutralize or at least mitigate those defenders’ ability to protect the rim.

As a result, the offensive team could possibly produce various scoring options by way of basketball cuts, dribble drive action and/or via kick passes to three-point shooters near the perimeter areas of the court.

Furthermore, opening up the low post areas could be particularly helpful for anundersized basketball team, which would typically have offensive challenges against defensive teams with more size and/or length.

sections.heading: Identification of man to man vs. zone defenses
sections.text:
The Horns offense could be implemented to determine if an opposing team is executing aman to man defenseor azone defense, especially at the beginning of aperiodor immediately following thesecondary breakportion of transition.

For example, if the offensive team sets up their Horns alignment but the post defenders do not swiftly match up with the high post players and at the same time, the wing defenders do not quickly match up with the offensive corner players, then it is very likely the opposing team is running a zone defense.

The YouTube video below by Coach Calls Timeout showcases an example of a Horns set vs. a 2-3 zone defense.
